texts.label: text
texts.orig: A teratogenic medication is one that can increase the risk of birth defects if taken at a particular time during pregnancy. In response to several requests, a CPRS Order Check was developed to remind providers when they prescribe a known or potential teratogen for a female patient of child-bearing potential. This reminder system is based on the FDA pregnancy categories (A, B, C, D, and X) and a set of additional criteria from First Data Bank that uses published literature and other available information. For lactating patients, the system also uses criteria to notify providers when a medication can cause serious side effects in a breastfeeding infant
texts.text: A teratogenic medication is one that can increase the risk of birth defects if taken at a particular time during pregnancy. In response to several requests, a CPRS Order Check was developed to remind providers when they prescribe a known or potential teratogen for a female patient of child-bearing potential. This reminder system is based on the FDA pregnancy categories (A, B, C, D, and X) and a set of additional criteria from First Data Bank that uses published literature and other available information. For lactating patients, the system also uses criteria to notify providers when a medication can cause serious side effects in a breastfeeding infant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This Teratogenic Medication Order Check project contains two patches, PXRM*2.0*22 and OR*3.0*357.
texts.text: This Teratogenic Medication Order Check project contains two patches, PXRM*2.0*22 and OR*3.0*357.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: PXRM*2.0*22 releases two new National Reminder Order Checks for placing Teratogenic Medications. A pre-installation routine will identify any previously identified components and rename them correctly, and the patch installation will create or overwrite VA-named, national reminder components
texts.text: PXRM*2.0*22 releases two new National Reminder Order Checks for placing Teratogenic Medications. A pre-installation routine will identify any previously identified components and rename them correctly, and the patch installation will create or overwrite VA-named, national reminder components

texts.orig: Check System release. To support these changes. ORKCHK5 has been updated to support the new API format for PXRMORCH. While testing this patch, a bug was found in the Order Check System, it was possible for the medium severity reminder order check to not show in CPRS if the order check system could not connect to First DataBank. ORCHECK, has been updated to fix this problem.
texts.text: Check System release. To support these changes. ORKCHK5 has been updated to support the new API format for PXRMORCH. While testing this patch, a bug was found in the Order Check System, it was possible for the medium severity reminder order check to not show in CPRS if the order check system could not connect to First DataBank. ORCHECK, has been updated to fix this problem.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Appendix A: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) about the Teratogenic Medications Order Check
texts.text: Appendix A: Frequently Asked Questions (FAQ) about the Teratogenic Medications Order Check

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A teratogenic medication is one that can increase the risk of birth defects if taken at a particular time during pregnancy. A
texts.text: A teratogenic medication is one that can increase the risk of birth defects if taken at a particular time during pregnancy. A

texts.label: text
texts.orig: is a medication that causes birth defects in humans. Examples include thalidomide (limb defects), isotretinoin (syndrome of defects including oral clefts), valproic acid (neural tube defects), and mycophenolate (facial clefts, anotia).  A
texts.text: is a medication that causes birth defects in humans. Examples include thalidomide (limb defects), isotretinoin (syndrome of defects including oral clefts), valproic acid (neural tube defects), and mycophenolate (facial clefts, anotia).  A

texts.label: text
texts.orig: is a medication that causes   malformations or other abnormal pregnancy outcomes in animal studies – these outcomes are more concerning if they occur at doses similar to human doses. Some medications increase the risk for pregnancy loss (miscarriage), growth abnormalities (e.g., growth restriction), or functional deficits (e.g., decreased fetal kidney function).  These are also important adverse reproductive effects.
texts.text: is a medication that causes   malformations or other abnormal pregnancy outcomes in animal studies – these outcomes are more concerning if they occur at doses similar to human doses. Some medications increase the risk for pregnancy loss (miscarriage), growth abnormalities (e.g., growth restriction), or functional deficits (e.g., decreased fetal kidney function).  These are also important adverse reproductive effects.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Q: What is the new Teratogenic Medication Order Check?
texts.text: Q: What is the new Teratogenic Medication Order Check?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In response to several requests, a CPRS Order Check was developed to remind providers when they prescribe a known or potential teratogen for a female patient of child-bearing potential. This reminder system is based on the FDA pregnancy categories (A, B, C, D, and X) and a set of additional criteria from First Data Bank that uses published literature and other available information.  For lactating patients, the system also uses criteria to notify providers when a medication can cause serious side effects in a breastfeeding infant
texts.text: In response to several requests, a CPRS Order Check was developed to remind providers when they prescribe a known or potential teratogen for a female patient of child-bearing potential. This reminder system is based on the FDA pregnancy categories (A, B, C, D, and X) and a set of additional criteria from First Data Bank that uses published literature and other available information.  For lactating patients, the system also uses criteria to notify providers when a medication can cause serious side effects in a breastfeeding infant

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Q: What are the FDA pregnancy categories?
texts.text: Q: What are the FDA pregnancy categories?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The FDA categories are a helpful system for distinguishing the relative teratogenic risk of a medicine based on studies in animals and experience in human pregnancy. As a health care provider, your awareness of these issues can help you better prescribe for and counsel your female patients of child-bearing potential. See the following table for category descriptions.
texts.text: The FDA categories are a helpful system for distinguishing the relative teratogenic risk of a medicine based on studies in animals and experience in human pregnancy. As a health care provider, your awareness of these issues can help you better prescribe for and counsel your female patients of child-bearing potential. See the following table for category descriptions.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Q: How will the Teratogenic Medication Order Check work?
texts.text: Q: How will the Teratogenic Medication Order Check work?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When a provider prescribes a medication with a pregnancy category D or X for a female patient between the ages of 18 and 50, the system will automatically provide an order check to ensure that you are aware of the reproductive risk associated with the medication. Certain additional medications with evidence of concern (e.g., some category C medications or FDA-unclassified medications) will also trigger an alert.
texts.text: When a provider prescribes a medication with a pregnancy category D or X for a female patient between the ages of 18 and 50, the system will automatically provide an order check to ensure that you are aware of the reproductive risk associated with the medication. Certain additional medications with evidence of concern (e.g., some category C medications or FDA-unclassified medications) will also trigger an alert.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Q: What do you do if you get an Order Check?
texts.text: Q: What do you do if you get an Order Check?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (1) Consider whether this medication is most appropriate for the patient’s condition or whether there are other appropriate options with less reproductive risk.
texts.text: (1) Consider whether this medication is most appropriate for the patient’s condition or whether there are other appropriate options with less reproductive risk.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (2) Counsel the patient about the risks and benefits of treatment should pregnancy occur.
texts.text: (2) Counsel the patient about the risks and benefits of treatment should pregnancy occur.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (3) Ask the patient about pregnancy planning and use of contraception. This risk/benefit discussion is an important one.
texts.text: (3) Ask the patient about pregnancy planning and use of contraception. This risk/benefit discussion is an important one.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: (4) For breastfeeding patients, consider whether there is an alternative medication with lower concentrations in breast milk or fewer risks for the infant and encourage the patient to discuss her medications with the infant’s pediatrician.  LactMed (
texts.text: (4) For breastfeeding patients, consider whether there is an alternative medication with lower concentrations in breast milk or fewer risks for the infant and encourage the patient to discuss her medications with the infant’s pediatrician.  LactMed (

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ) is an online resource from the NIH that provides available information about use of medications during lactation.
texts.text: ) is an online resource from the NIH that provides available information about use of medications during lactation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Where can I get information about the pros/cons of continuing use of a teratogenic medication during pregnancy?
texts.text: Where can I get information about the pros/cons of continuing use of a teratogenic medication during pregnancy?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deciding whether to continue a potential or known teratogen during pregnancy is complicated.  Not treating or under-treating a pregnant woman’s medical condition can be riskier to the fetus than the medication itself.  It is helpful to read the pregnancy subsection of drug labeling, which provides information about the reproductive risks of medications.  Current labeling can be found at Drugs@FDA or at
texts.text: Deciding whether to continue a potential or known teratogen during pregnancy is complicated.  Not treating or under-treating a pregnant woman’s medical condition can be riskier to the fetus than the medication itself.  It is helpful to read the pregnancy subsection of drug labeling, which provides information about the reproductive risks of medications.  Current labeling can be found at Drugs@FDA or at

texts.label: text
texts.orig: .  Reprotox is an online resource that summarizes available information about the reproductive risks of medications.  Reprotox may be available online through your facility’s library and will be available through the VACO library in the near future.  The Organization of Teratology Information Services has fact sheets available online about medications and their risks during pregnancy (
texts.text: .  Reprotox is an online resource that summarizes available information about the reproductive risks of medications.  Reprotox may be available online through your facility’s library and will be available through the VACO library in the near future.  The Organization of Teratology Information Services has fact sheets available online about medications and their risks during pregnancy (

texts.label: text
texts.orig: ).  Your local pharmacist and women’s health providers can provide more information.
texts.text: ).  Your local pharmacist and women’s health providers can provide more information.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Q: How can I help my patients avoid unintended pregnancy?
texts.text: Q: How can I help my patients avoid unintended pregnancy?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: About 50% of pregnancies in the United States are unplanned. During patient encounters, ask women of child-bearing potential (age 15 to 50) about their plans for pregnancy and need for contraception.
texts.text: About 50% of pregnancies in the United States are unplanned. During patient encounters, ask women of child-bearing potential (age 15 to 50) about their plans for pregnancy and need for contraception.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The most effective reversible contraceptives are the IUDs (Mirena or Paragard) and subdermal implants (Nexplanon, previously Implanon). These contraceptives are available through the VA prosthetics department and can be inserted by a local trained women’s health provider. Oral contraceptive pills and emergency contraception are on the VA formulary, and condoms are available through the VA. Female Veterans using contraceptives other than IUDs and implants should be advised to keep a supply of emergency contraception on hand in case of unprotected intercourse or contraceptive failure.
texts.text: The most effective reversible contraceptives are the IUDs (Mirena or Paragard) and subdermal implants (Nexplanon, previously Implanon). These contraceptives are available through the VA prosthetics department and can be inserted by a local trained women’s health provider. Oral contraceptive pills and emergency contraception are on the VA formulary, and condoms are available through the VA. Female Veterans using contraceptives other than IUDs and implants should be advised to keep a supply of emergency contraception on hand in case of unprotected intercourse or contraceptive failure.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Q: When will the Order Check be available?
texts.text: Q: When will the Order Check be available?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The Order Check will come as a VistA Clinical Reminders patch, PXRM*2*22.  At the time of this message, release is anticipated during the fourth quarter of calendar year 2012.
texts.text: The Order Check will come as a VistA Clinical Reminders patch, PXRM*2*22.  At the time of this message, release is anticipated during the fourth quarter of calendar year 2012.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Q: How often will this Order Check appear?
texts.text: Q: How often will this Order Check appear?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A: Clinicians will be notified every time any category D or X medications or certain other medications with evidence for concern (e.g., some category C medications or FDA-unclassified medications) are ordered for a female patient of child-bearing potential who is without documentation of medical inability to become pregnant.
texts.text: A: Clinicians will be notified every time any category D or X medications or certain other medications with evidence for concern (e.g., some category C medications or FDA-unclassified medications) are ordered for a female patient of child-bearing potential who is without documentation of medical inability to become pregnant.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Q: Will the Order Check display even if the patient has no chance of becoming pregnant, such as post-hysterectomy?
texts.text: Q: Will the Order Check display even if the patient has no chance of becoming pregnant, such as post-hysterectomy?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: No. The Order Check is designed to recognize several exclusion criteria that can be generally described as “medical inability to conceive.”  For example, patients for whom the appropriate ICD-9
texts.text: No. The Order Check is designed to recognize several exclusion criteria that can be generally described as “medical inability to conceive.”  For example, patients for whom the appropriate ICD-9

texts.label: text
texts.orig: code for hysterectomy is recorded will not trigger an alert.  Other ICD-9
texts.text: code for hysterectomy is recorded will not trigger an alert.  Other ICD-9

texts.label: text
texts.orig: exclusion criteria include menopausal state and IUD use.
texts.text: exclusion criteria include menopausal state and IUD use.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Q: Can clinicians add information to the electronic health record that should exclude a patient from this Order Check?
texts.text: Q: Can clinicians add information to the electronic health record that should exclude a patient from this Order Check?

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Yes, each site can define additional situations or “Health Factors” when the Order Check does not apply. The local application support team can add these Health Factors to the Reminder Term called “VA-TERATOGENIC MEDICATIONS ORDER CHECK EXCLUSIONS (TERM).”  The support team can also suggest to the Women’s Health Program Office that a Health Factor be added as part of a subsequent national update.
texts.text: Yes, each site can define additional situations or “Health Factors” when the Order Check does not apply. The local application support team can add these Health Factors to the Reminder Term called “VA-TERATOGENIC MEDICATIONS ORDER CHECK EXCLUSIONS (TERM).”  The support team can also suggest to the Women’s Health Program Office that a Health Factor be added as part of a subsequent national update.

texts.text: At the time of this message, the transition from ICD-9 to ICD-10 has not yet taken place

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The FDA published a proposed rule in the May 29, 2008, Federal Register that proposes new labeling regulations for prescription drugs. These regulations, once implemented, will eliminate the pregnancy categories from drug labeling. When a final rule publishes, VHA will determine how to best update this order check process so that it will remain both current and useful to VA providers.
texts.text: The FDA published a proposed rule in the May 29, 2008, Federal Register that proposes new labeling regulations for prescription drugs. These regulations, once implemented, will eliminate the pregnancy categories from drug labeling. When a final rule publishes, VHA will determine how to best update this order check process so that it will remain both current and useful to VA providers.

table_cells.text:
Table 1.   FDA Pregnancy categories 
(language summarized from 21CFR201.57)

Adequate and well-controlled (AWC) studies in pregnant women have failed to demonstrate a risk to the fetus in the first trimester of pregnancy (and there is no evidence of a risk in later trimesters).

Animal reproduction studies have failed to demonstrate a risk to the fetus and there are no AWC studies in pregnant women, OR animal studies demonstrate a risk and AWC studies in pregnant women have not during the first trimester (and there is no evidence of risk in later trimesters).

Animal reproduction studies have shown an adverse effect on the fetus, there are no AWC studies in humans, AND the benefits from the use of the drug in pregnant women may be acceptable despite its potential risks. OR animal studies have not been conducted and there are no AWC studies in humans.

There is positive evidence of human fetal risk based on adverse reaction data from investigational or marketing experience or studies in humans, BUT the potential benefits from the use of the drug in pregnant women may be acceptable despite its potential risks (for example, if the drug is needed in a life-threatening situation or serious disease for which safer drugs cannot be used or are ineffective).

Studies in animals or humans have demonstrated fetal abnormalities OR there is positive evidence of fetal risk based on adverse reaction reports from investigational or marketing experience, or both, AND the risk of the use of the drug in a pregnant woman clearly outweighs any possible benefit (for example, safer drugs or other forms of therapy are available).

grid.text:
Table 1.   FDA Pregnancy categories 
(language summarized from 21CFR201.57)

Table 1.   FDA Pregnancy categories 
(language summarized from 21CFR201.57)

Adequate and well-controlled (AWC) studies in pregnant women have failed to demonstrate a risk to the fetus in the first trimester of pregnancy (and there is no evidence of a risk in later trimesters).

Animal reproduction studies have failed to demonstrate a risk to the fetus and there are no AWC studies in pregnant women, OR animal studies demonstrate a risk and AWC studies in pregnant women have not during the first trimester (and there is no evidence of risk in later trimesters).

Animal reproduction studies have shown an adverse effect on the fetus, there are no AWC studies in humans, AND the benefits from the use of the drug in pregnant women may be acceptable despite its potential risks. OR animal studies have not been conducted and there are no AWC studies in humans.

There is positive evidence of human fetal risk based on adverse reaction data from investigational or marketing experience or studies in humans, BUT the potential benefits from the use of the drug in pregnant women may be acceptable despite its potential risks (for example, if the drug is needed in a life-threatening situation or serious disease for which safer drugs cannot be used or are ineffective).

Studies in animals or humans have demonstrated fetal abnormalities OR there is positive evidence of fetal risk based on adverse reaction reports from investigational or marketing experience, or both, AND the risk of the use of the drug in a pregnant woman clearly outweighs any possible benefit (for example, safer drugs or other forms of therapy are available).
